---
title: "West Nile virus surveillance in California"
subtitle: "MPH thesis — spatial and spatiotemporal modeling of mosquito-pool infection"
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Spatial epidemiology]{.tag} [Bayesian modeling]{.tag} [Spatiotemporal]{.tag} [Surveillance data]{.tag} [Public health]{.tag} [R]{.tag}
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Status:** in progress. The methods and pipeline are built and tested on
simulated data; the analysis on real surveillance data is pending data-use and
review requirements. No results are reported here yet.
:::

## Problem

West Nile virus is monitored in California largely through mosquito surveillance:
agencies set traps, collect mosquitoes, sort them into pools by species and
location, and test those pools for the virus. The resulting data is the earliest
signal we have for local WNV risk — but it is surveillance data, not a designed
experiment. Traps are not placed at random, effort varies across counties and
years, and a positive pool depends both on how much virus is circulating and on
how hard and where anyone looked.

The question I'm working on: across California, how do environmental conditions
and surveillance effort relate to WNV infection rate in mosquito pools, once you
account for the fact that the data has spatial and temporal structure and uneven
detection?

## Approach

I frame the outcome as an infection rate in *Culex* mosquito pools — the
probability that a mosquito carries the virus, estimated from pooled test
results rather than treating each pool as a simple positive/negative. The
analytic unit is the county-month, over a multi-year study period covering
California and a buffer into neighboring states.

The design takes surveillance effort and detection seriously as part of the data-
generating process rather than a nuisance to be dropped. Effort enters the
model; the estimand is defined up front; and spatial confounding is addressed
directly rather than assumed away.

The work moves in stages:

1. **Exploratory spatial data analysis (ESDA)** — describe the spatial and
   temporal pattern, test for clustering, and locate hot spots before fitting
   anything.
2. **Covariate assembly** — build a reproducible environmental data warehouse on
   the county-month grid.
3. **Hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling** — fit Bayesian models that share
   information across space and time and separate signal from surveillance
   artifact.

## Methods and tools

- **Outcome.** Pooled-prevalence infection rate for WNV in *Culex* vectors,
  estimated from mosquito-pool test data with pool sizes accounted for.
- **Structure.** County-month observations across California (plus a buffer of
  neighboring-state counties) over a multi-year period.
- **Environmental covariates.** Vegetation indices (NDVI/EVI), land surface
  temperature, climate and drought measures, land cover, and irrigation, among
  others — assembled and validated on the analytic grid.
- **Exploratory spatial analysis.** Global and local autocorrelation
  (Moran's I, Getis-Ord Gi\*), emerging hot-spot analysis, and space-time
  description.
- **Modeling.** Bayesian hierarchical spatiotemporal models — a conditional
  autoregressive (BYM2-type) spatial structure with temporal terms and
  distributed-lag climate effects, fit with established spatial-modeling engines.
  A spatial-confounding adjustment ("spatial+") and with/without-spatial
  sensitivity analyses are part of the plan.
- **Stack.** R for the analysis and modeling; a SQL-backed ETL pipeline for the
  data warehouse; version-controlled and reproducible throughout.

## Status

The pipeline and modeling harness are built and have been stress-tested on
simulated data — feasibility checks, pathology tests, and a model-specification
study to choose the analytic model before touching the real outcome. The
simulation study uses real public covariate structure with simulated outcomes
carrying a known effect, then checks whether each candidate model's 95%
intervals cover that effect at the stated rate, across data-generating scenarios
that vary temporal autocorrelation, overdispersion, and positivity. One clear
finding so far: omitting seasonal and month-to-month temporal structure produces
substantial under-coverage of the uncertainty intervals — and the point
estimates alone do not reveal the problem. That result is about the modeling
framework, not about WNV.

The analysis on actual surveillance data is gated on data-use and review
requirements, so no substantive results are reported here. The framing is
deliberately provisional: this page describes the design, not findings.

## What this demonstrates

- Building a reproducible environmental-and-surveillance data pipeline from
  heterogeneous public sources.
- Treating surveillance effort and detection as part of the model rather than
  ignoring them.
- Applied Bayesian hierarchical and spatiotemporal modeling, with spatial
  confounding handled explicitly.
- Validating a modeling approach on simulated data before committing to it.

*Links to the thesis document, methods write-ups, and any preprint will be added
here as they become available.*
